articles.title: Reducing Doom Loops with Final Token Preference Optimization
articles.summary: Reducing Doom Loops with Final Token Preference Optimization is a method that targets repetitive looping in inference by identifying the exact token that begins a loop, training the model to prefer coherent alternatives at that single position, and leaving the rest of the distribution largely untouched. This approach adapts Antislop, training on chosen/rejected pairs that represent a single completion token using Final Token Preference Optimization. The method has shown promising results in reducing looping rates for hard math and coding prompts.

articles.title: Jim's TrueType QR Code Font
articles.summary: Jim's TrueType QR Code Font generates an experimental OpenType font that turns bracket-delimited text into a QR Code symbol while leaving surrounding text readable. The fonts are generated rather than hand-authored and use Byte mode, Printable ASCII input, and fixed mask pattern to achieve this. This project uses uv for Python dependency management and can be run with different base fonts to generate Reed-Solomon circuits or placeholder zero parity.

articles.title: Mechanized type inference for record concatenation
articles.summary: Mechanized type inference for record concatenation is a challenging problem that has been around since 1991, when Mitchell Wand published an algorithm for it. Despite being not a research problem, no language in existence currently implements this algorithm, which makes it difficult to mechanize and translate well into code. This post aims to formalize the algorithm in three different ways, including a declarative natural deduction semantics, an algorithmic semantics using constraint generation and resolution, and a reference Haskell implementation that is approximately 400 lines of code long.

articles.title: You shouldn't trust Trusted Publishing
articles.summary: Trusted Publishing involves machine-to-machine trust, using OpenID Connect federation to authenticate packages on platforms like PyPI and npm. This approach combines long-lived credentials with short-lived publishing credentials, making it harder for users to manage their own credentials but still allowing large projects to maintain control over their identity. Despite its complexities, Trusted Publishing has been a successful strategy in packaging ecosystems, providing a convenient way for users to publish packages while maintaining their source identity.

articles.summary: The Popup That Says the Quiet Part Out Loud Part of Apple's ongoing war on users, with a key finding that the company is now allowing third-party cloud services to host AI models and data, which raises concerns about user privacy and control. The popup, which appears when using shape generation in iWork or similar features in Freeform, tells users their data is being sent to Google Cloud, not Apple's servers. This shift marks a significant change in how Apple handles AI and data, as it now relies on third-party cloud services for certain tasks, potentially compromising user trust and control over their own data.

articles.title: 98% Isn't Much
articles.summary: A website that uses fancy new browser features and works for 98% of the population may not work for a significant portion of its visitors, such as those with older browsers or devices. This is because the feature's implementation may not be robust enough to handle edge cases, leading to performance issues. In contrast, a website that prioritizes basic expectations, like providing a good experience, will likely have a more reliable user base.

articles.title: Signed Integers By Default
articles.summary: Signed integers by default have become a contentious issue in programming languages, with some designers opting to use unsigned integers as the standard choice. However, this decision has significant implications for coding style and consistency. A language designer's article on C3 language highlights the trade-offs involved in choosing signed or unsigned integers as the default integer kind, emphasizing that signed works better in practice than unsigned theory. The author argues that using unsigned types can lead to infinite loops and out-of-bounds errors due to a fundamental misunderstanding of how integers work on the machine.

articles.title: Why false sharing alignment should be 128 bytes on x64
articles.summary: False sharing can occur when two atomic variables reside on the same cache line, causing coordination between CPUs that doesn't happen per-address and leading to inefficient use of cache lines. To mitigate this issue, developers can separate atomics by making them reside in different cache lines using alignment directives such as Rust's #[repr(align(N))] or C11 _Alignas(N) or GCC/Clang __attribute__((aligned(N))). This simple solution can improve performance on x86_64 architectures and is recommended by experts.
